structured_choices.data_source: Electronic health records (EHRs) from a single hospital system spanning 5 years (60 months). All patient encounters with complete structured data (demographics, clinical codes, comorbidities, admission type, length of stay) and clinical note text are included. Records are included only if a patient has a documented 30-day readmission outcome (primary outcome window). Data quality: missing structured fields are flagged and handled via domain-appropriate imputation or exclusion per field; clinical notes with length < 50 tokens are excluded; patients with <1 prior encounter are excluded to allow longitudinal history extraction.
structured_choices.label_definition: Binary outcome: unplanned readmission within 30 days of discharge (1) vs. no readmission within 30 days (0). Readmissions are identified via discharge codes and admission records; only index admissions (first admission in a 60-day window) are used as the prediction point to avoid dependent outcomes. Label is determined at discharge, not at prediction time.
structured_choices.split_method: Temporal split with stratification by hospital ward/department and readmission rate to preserve population composition across folds. Data is ordered chronologically by admission date. The first 36 months (months 1–36) form the training set (n ≈ 60,000); months 37–48 form the validation set (n ≈ 20,000); months 49–60 form the held-out test set (n ≈ 20,000). Within each split, stratified sampling ensures that the distribution of readmission rates and department membership is preserved. Temporal ordering prevents information leakage: no future patient data is used to predict past outcomes. Stratification controls for confounding by ward practices and acuity differences across time periods.
structured_choices.preprocessing_fit_point: All preprocessing transformers (numerical scalers, categorical encoders, text tokenizers, word embeddings) are fit exclusively on the training set (months 1–36). Fitted transformers are applied identically to validation and test sets without refitting. Clinical note preprocessing (lowercasing, tokenization, removal of PHI patterns) uses a fixed, domain-standard pipeline fitted once on training notes. Word embeddings (e.g., BioWordVec or domain-specific embeddings trained on clinical corpora, not on the study data) are pre-trained external embeddings; no fitting occurs on study data.
structured_choices.features: Structured features: demographic (age, sex, race/ethnicity), clinical (Charlson comorbidity index, admission type, primary diagnosis code, secondary diagnosis codes up to 25 most frequent), utilization (prior ED visits in past 12 months, prior admissions in past 12 months, length of stay, ICU admission flag). Unstructured features: discharge summary, progress notes, and nursing notes are concatenated; extracted via TF-IDF bag-of-words (top 500 terms) and via a pre-trained clinical BERT encoder (768-dim embeddings). Temporal features: days since last admission, count of admissions in prior 6/12 months. All numerical features are standardized (z-score) on training data. No derived features encode the label or depend on post-discharge events.
structured_choices.model: Multi-task deep learning architecture: a shared input layer processes structured features (dense 128-dim layer with ReLU) and unstructured features (clinical BERT embeddings passed through a transformer encoder with 2 attention heads, then max-pooled). The two representations are concatenated and passed through a 3-layer fully connected network (256 → 128 → 64 dims, ReLU activation, 0.3 dropout between layers) with a final sigmoid output for readmission prediction. A secondary auxiliary task (predicting length of stay as a regression task) shares the first two hidden layers to regularize shared representations. Model is trained with Adam optimizer (lr=0.001), binary cross-entropy loss for readmission, MSE loss for length of stay (weighted 0.8:0.2), and early stopping on validation readmission AUROC.
structured_choices.baseline: Logistic regression trained on structured features only: demographics, Charlson index, admission type, primary/secondary diagnoses (one-hot encoded), utilization variables, and temporal variables. The baseline receives identical feature engineering (standardization, categorical encoding) as the multi-task model. Baseline hyperparameter tuning (regularization strength, class weight balancing) is performed on the validation set using grid search over L1/L2 penalties (0.001, 0.01, 0.1) and class weights (balanced, 1:2, 1:5 readmission:non-readmission) with AUROC as the selection metric. This ensures the baseline is given equivalent tuning effort and evaluated under identical conditions. The baseline represents the current standard practice (structured data only) and is the appropriate null hypothesis.
structured_choices.primary_metric: Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC). Justification: AUROC is insensitive to class imbalance and threshold choice, making it suitable for a clinically-motivated threshold-flexible comparison. However, because stakeholders prioritize both sensitivity and specificity (preventing both false positives—unnecessary interventions—and false negatives—missed high-risk patients), AUROC is supplemented by: (1) threshold-specific metrics at a clinically-calibrated operating point chosen to balance sensitivity and specificity. Secondary metrics: F1-score at the validation-optimized threshold, sensitivity and specificity at the operating point, and calibration curve (expected calibration error) to ensure predicted probabilities are reliable for clinical decision-making.
secondary_metrics: Sensitivity (recall) at the validation-selected threshold — captures rate of correctly identified readmission cases
Specificity at the validation-selected threshold — captures rate of correctly identified non-readmission cases
Precision at the validation-selected threshold — relevant for resource allocation (% of flagged patients truly at risk)
F1-score — harmonic mean of precision and recall, useful for imbalanced settings
Expected Calibration Error (ECE) — ensures predicted probabilities reflect true risk for clinical use
Readmission rate stratified by risk quartile — evaluates whether the model produces meaningful risk stratification
structured_choices.model_selection_data: Hyperparameter tuning and early stopping are performed using the validation set (months 37–48, n ≈ 20,000). Grid search over multi-task model architecture (number of attention heads, shared layer depth, auxiliary task weight), learning rate, and dropout rates is conducted on validation AUROC. Early stopping is triggered if validation AUROC does not improve for 10 consecutive epochs. The test set (months 49–60) is held entirely out of model selection and is evaluated exactly once at the end with the final model.
structured_choices.test_set_policy: The test set is not accessed during model development, hyperparameter tuning, or any form of model selection. After final model training, a single evaluation is performed on the test set to report primary and secondary metrics. No re-tuning or threshold adjustment is performed after test set evaluation. Confidence intervals (95%, computed via 5,000 bootstrap resamples of the test set with stratification by readmission outcome) are reported for all metrics.
structured_choices.confound_controls: Temporal split controls for confounding by hospital practices evolving over time (e.g., changes in discharge protocols, coding practices, or staffing). Stratification by ward/department within each split controls for department-specific readmission rates and acuity differences. Confounding by patient case mix is minimized by including comprehensive comorbidity (Charlson) and acuity indicators (ICU status, LOS). Sensitivity analysis: performance is separately evaluated within each of the 3–5 largest departments to assess whether the multi-task advantage holds uniformly or is department-specific. Post-hoc analysis: logistic regression coefficients and feature importance (via SHAP values for the multi-task model) are inspected for clinical plausibility; unexpected strong associations (e.g., a feature predictive only due to bias rather than clinical signal) are flagged and investigated.

design_narrative:
## Experiment Design: Multi-Task Deep Learning vs. Logistic Regression for Patient Readmission Prediction

### 1. Data and Cohort Definition

We would use 60 months of electronic health records from a single hospital system, comprising approximately 100,000 patient encounters. The cohort includes all index admissions (first admission in a rolling 60-day window) with complete structured data fields and at least one clinical note. We exclude records with missing critical structured variables (e.g., admission type, primary diagnosis) and clinical notes shorter than 50 tokens. The outcome is binary: unplanned readmission within 30 days of discharge (yes/no), determined at the time of discharge using discharge codes and subsequent admission records.

Data quality is managed as follows: missing comorbidity flags are imputed as absent (conservative assumption); missing utilization variables (prior ED visits, prior admissions) are imputed as zero; sparse categorical variables are grouped into an 'other' category if they represent <1% of training data. Any records with missing outcome labels are excluded.

### 2. Temporal and Stratified Split

We employ a temporal split to prevent information leakage and to reflect the chronological nature of clinical decision-making. Months 1–36 (training, n ≈ 60,000), months 37–48 (validation, n ≈ 20,000), and months 49–60 (test, n ≈ 20,000) are separated by admission date.

Within each split, stratified sampling is applied by hospital ward/department and readmission rate quartile to ensure that the distribution of patient populations is preserved across folds. This stratification controls for time-varying confounds such as changes in hospital practices, staffing, or discharge protocols that might systematically affect readmission rates across the study period.

The temporal ordering ensures that no future information is used to predict past outcomes, and the stratification ensures that systematic differences in acuity or department culture do not inflate or deflate performance estimates.

### 3. Feature Engineering and Preprocessing

All numerical features (age, prior admission counts, length of stay) are standardized using z-score normalization fit on the training set only, then applied to validation and test. Categorical variables (admission type, primary diagnosis, ICU status) are one-hot encoded with categories fitted on the training set; unseen categories in validation/test are mapped to zero.

Structured features include: demographics (age, sex, race/ethnicity), comorbidity burden (Charlson Comorbidity Index), admission characteristics (type, length of stay, ICU status), diagnosis codes (primary and up to 25 most-frequent secondary codes), and utilization history (ED visits and admissions in the prior 6 and 12 months).

Clinical notes (discharge summaries, progress notes, nursing notes) are preprocessed via lowercasing, tokenization, and removal of personally identifiable information (PHI) patterns using a fixed, domain-standard pipeline. Two unstructured representations are extracted: (1) TF-IDF bag-of-words (top 500 terms by training set frequency) and (2) contextual embeddings using a pre-trained clinical BERT model (768 dimensions). These representations are concatenated.

All preprocessing transformers are fit exclusively on the training set; validation and test data are transformed using these fitted transformers without modification.

### 4. Models and Baseline

**Multi-task Deep Learning Model:** A shared architecture processes structured features (dense 128-dim layer, ReLU) and unstructured features (clinical BERT embeddings → 2-head transformer encoder → max pooling). Representations are concatenated and passed through a 3-layer fully connected network (256 → 128 → 64 dims, ReLU, 0.3 dropout). A secondary auxiliary task (length-of-stay regression) shares the first two hidden layers to regularize learned representations. The model is trained with Adam (lr=0.001), binary cross-entropy for readmission, and MSE for LOS (0.8:0.2 weight). Early stopping is triggered if validation AUROC does not improve for 10 epochs.

**Baseline: Logistic Regression on Structured Features.** A logistic regression model trained on demographics, Charlson index, admission type, diagnosis codes, and utilization features receives identical preprocessing and feature engineering as the multi-task model. Hyperparameter tuning (L1/L2 regularization strength, class weights) is conducted via grid search on the validation set using AUROC. This ensures the baseline is given equivalent tuning effort and represents the current standard practice.

### 5. Evaluation Strategy

Model selection is performed exclusively on the validation set (months 37–48) using AUROC as the criterion. The test set is held out and evaluated exactly once at the end.

**Primary Metric:** Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic Curve (AUROC) on the test set. AUROC is chosen because it is insensitive to class imbalance and threshold choice, providing a summary measure of model discrimination. The stakeholder priority of minimizing both false positives and false negatives is captured by AUROC, which rewards the model for maintaining high true positive and true negative rates across operating points.

**Secondary Metrics on the Test Set:**
- Sensitivity and specificity at a clinically-calibrated operating point (selected on validation data to achieve ≥90% sensitivity, minimizing missed high-risk cases)
- Precision at the operating point (informing resource allocation)
- F1-score (harmonic mean of precision and recall)
- Expected Calibration Error (ECE) (ensuring predicted probabilities are reliable for risk stratification)
- Readmission rate by risk quartile (assessing clinical utility of risk stratification)

95% confidence intervals are computed via 5,000 bootstrap resamples of the test set.

### 6. Controls and Confounds

Temporal stratification controls for time-varying hospital practice changes. Department-level stratification controls for department-specific readmission rates and acuity differences. Sensitivity analysis evaluates model performance separately within the 3–5 largest departments to assess generalization. Post-hoc inspection of logistic regression coefficients and SHAP feature importance scores ensures clinical plausibility and flags unexpected proxy signals.

### 7. Scope and Claims

This design establishes whether the multi-task model achieves higher discrimination (AUROC) and better risk stratification than logistic regression in this hospital's population during the study period. The temporal split limits claims to this institution; external generalization requires evaluation on held-out sites. The design does not establish causality or the mechanism by which unstructured data improves prediction.
